trans-unit.source:
This is Part B of the survey.

In Part B the questions are classroom specific. If you teach more than one class of students included in this project, you will complete Part B separately for each classroom.

If you teach a single subject, please complete the questions that relate to other subjects to the best of your knowledge or consult with a colleague for those details only.

When you are ready to begin, select the “Next” button.

Thank you!
How many classrooms do you teach that contain students participating in this research project?
Is there another adult who is paid to help you in this classroom (e.g., teaching assistant, co-lead teacher, etc.)?
How many students are enrolled in this classroom?
How many children of each gender are in this class?
As of today's date, how many children in this class are each of the following age levels?
Do you currently teach a multigrade class?
If yes, what grade levels are included in the class you teach? (select all that apply)
What grade level do you teach?
What languages are spoken by students in this class (select all that apply)?
What languages do you or your assistant teacher speak in the classroom (select all that apply)?
About how many children in this classroom are BELOW grade level in their reading skills?
About how many children in this classroom are ABOVE grade level in their reading skills?
About how many children in this classroom are BELOW grade level in their math skills?
About how many children in this classroom are ABOVE grade level in their math skills?
How often each week do children in this class usually work on lessons or projects in Reading and Language Arts, whether as a whole class, in small groups, or in individualized arrangements ?
How much time per day do children in this class usually work on lessons or projects in Reading and Language Arts, whether as a whole class, in small groups, or in individualized arrangements?
How often each week do children in this class usually work on lessons or projects in Mathematics, whether as a whole class, in small groups, or in individualized arrangements?
How much time per day do children in this class usually work on lessons or projects in Mathematics, whether as a whole class, in small groups, or in individualized arrangements?
Think about the families of the children in this class who you have taught this school year. For about how many families are each of the following statements true?
Families seem overwhelmed by trying to support their children's learning.
Families are doing their part to support children's learning.
Please indicate how satisfied or dissatisfied you have been with the following this school year.
Communication between you and your students.

Thank you to you and your child for participating in this research project!
The following survey will take approximately 20-30 minutes to complete and will ask questions about your child.

When answering the questions, please think about your child who is participating in this research project. If you have more than one child participating in the research project, you will complete this survey separately for each participating child.

Some questions will note a specific timeframe to consider when answering. If no timeframe is indicated, you can think about how the answer applies to your child in general.

We appreciate you answering as completely and accurately as possible with the information you have available to you. If you have questions about this survey, please reach out to the research coordinator.

When you are ready to begin, select the “start survey” button.

Thank you!
This section asks general information questions and questions about your child's health.
<block><b><font face="Verdana" size="4">This section asks general information questions and questions about your child's health.
Please indicate your relationship to the child participating in this research project.
Please specify your relationship to the child.
Please indicate for how long you have been caring for this child.
What is your child's current age in years?
What is your child's CURRENT height? (please estimate to the best of your knowledge)
What is your child's CURRENT weight? (please estimate to the best of your knowledge)
Was your child born early (more than three weeks before due date)?
How much did your child weigh at birth? (if you do not know, leave blank)
In general, how would you describe your child's health?
How would you describe the condition of your child's teeth?
DURING THE PAST WEEK, on how many days did your child exercise, play a sport, or participate in physical activity (either in school or outside of school) for at least 60 minutes?
DURING THE PAST 12 MONTHS, has your child had FREQUENT or CHRONIC difficulty with any of the following?
Breathing or other respiratory problems (such as wheezing or shortness of breath)
Eating or swallowing because of a health condition
Digesting food, including stomach/intestinal problems, constipation, or diarrhea
Repeated or chronic physical pain, including headaches or other back or body pain
Does your child have any of the following?
Serious difficulty concentrating, remembering, or making decisions because of a physical, mental, or emotional condition
Serious difficulty walking or climbing stairs
Blindness or problems with seeing, even when wearing glasses
Has a doctor or other health care provider, or educator EVER told you that your child has any of the following:
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have Behavioral or Conduct Problems?
Are the current Behavioral or Conduct Problems, Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have Developmental Delay?
Are the current Developmental Delays, Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have an Intellectual Disability?
Is the current Intellectual Disability, Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have a Speech or other language disorder?
Is the current Speech or other language disorder, Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have a Learning Disability?
Is the current Learning Disability, Mild, Moderate, or Severe?

Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
(includes Asperger's Disorder or Pervasive Developmental Disorder (PDD))
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have an Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) (includes Asperger's Disorder or Pervasive Developmental Disorder (PDD))?
Is the current Autism Spectrum Disorder (includes Asperger's Disorder or Pervasive Developmental Disorder), Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
Attention Deficit Disorder or Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADD or ADHD)
Does your child <b>CURRENTLY</b > have Attention Deficit Disorder or Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADD or ADHD)?
Is the current Attention Deficit Disorder or Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADD or ADHD), Mild, Moderate, or Severe?
What was your child's sex at birth (i.e. on their birth certificate)?

The following set of questions asks about gender identity.

Would you like to answer these additional questions about gender?
(Note: your child will NOT be asked these questions)
What is your child's current gender identification?
Over the last year, how frequently did your child engage in behaviors or express preferences that are more commonly associated with girls?
Over the last year, how frequently did your child state that they are, or want to be, a girl?
Over the last year, how frequently did your child engage in behaviors or express preferences that are more commonly associated with boys?
Over the last year, how frequently did your child state that they are, or want to be, a boy?
Has your child begun to show signs of puberty (even if very early)?
For each question, please select the answer that best describes what is happening to your child right now.
Would you say that your child's growth in height (getting taller):
Has not yet begun to spurt ("spurt" means more growth than usual)
Has definitely started to happen but is not finished
Seems completed (child is about as tall as she is going to get)
How about the growth of body hair (e.g. under arms)? Would you say your child's body hair:
Has definitely started growing but is not finished
Seems completed (child has as much body hair as she is going to get)
Have you noticed any skin changes, especially pimples?
Skin changes have definitely started but are not finished
Have your child's breasts begun to grow?
Breast growth has definitely started but is not finished
Has your child begun to menstruate?
If yes, how old was your child when she first got her period?
For each question, please select the answer that best describes what is happening to your child right now.
Would you say that your child's growth in height (getting taller):
Has not yet begun to spurt ("spurt" means more growth than usual)
Has definitely started to happen but is not finished
Seems completed (child is about as tall as he is going to get)
How about the growth of body hair (e.g. under arms)? Would you say your child's body hair:
Has definitely started growing but is not finished
Seems completed (child has as much body hair as he is going to get)
Have you noticed any skin changes, especially pimples?
Skin changes have definitely started but are not finished
Has your child's voice started to change?
Voice change is definitely happening but is not finished
Has your child started to grow facial hair (beard or mustache)?
Not yet started growing facial hair
Has barely started growing facial hair
Hair growth has definitely started (enough to shave)
Probably grows now as fast as it will ever grow
Please respond to each statement about your child's sleep habits.
Usually (5 or more times per week)
Your child had a hard time falling asleep (did not fall asleep within 20 minutes of going to bed).
Your child had difficulty sleeping through the night (woke up once or more during the night).
Your child went to bed at about the same time on the weeknights (Monday-Friday).
DURING THE PAST WEEK, about how many hours of sleep per night did your child usually get on the weeknights (Monday-Friday)?
<block><b><font face=Verdana size=4>This section asks about your child's behavior and social interaction.

For each item, please mark "Not at all", "Not very well", "Somewhat well", or "Very well" to indicate how well the sentence applies to your child. Please answer all items as best you can even if you are not absolutely certain.
Your child has difficulty staying still or shows periods of over-activity.
Your child often complains about feeling sick (ex: headache, stomach ache).
Your child avoids others or prefers to spend time alone.
Your child is often worried or anxious.
You child often seems sad, depressed, or down.
Your child gets along with other children.
Your child gets distracted easily or has trouble staying on task.
Your child often shows dishonest behavior (e.g. lying, cheating).
Your child has taken things without permission from home, school, or elsewhere.
Please indicate how well each statement describes your child.
Your child accepts things when they do not go their way.
Your child copes well with failure.
Your child can calm down when excited.
Your child shares things with others.
Your child is helpful to others.
Your child exhibits good self-control in responding.
Please indicate how well each statement describes your child.
Your child is curious to investigate and understand new things.
Your child gives unique responses that go beyond those of other children.
Your child likes to ask many questions.
Your child completes their own work without being asked or reminded to do so.
Your child works diligently when trying to complete tasks or chores.
Your child likes to finish tasks properly.
If your child is told to do a chore, they do it.
<block><b><font face=Verdana size=4>This section asks questions related to learning.

If your child is in primary/elementary school (first grade or higher), think about the time before they started primary/elementary school. If your child has not yet started primary/elementary school, think about what happens right now.

How often did/do you or someone else in your home do the following activities with your child?
Play with alphabet toys (e.g., blocks with letters of the alphabet)
Talk about things you had done
Talk about what you had read
Say counting rhymes or sing counting songs
Play with number toys (e.g., blocks with numbers)
Play games involving shapes (e.g., shape sorting toys, puzzles)
Play with building blocks or construction toys
Measure or weigh things (e.g., when cooking)
Did your child attend an early childhood education program or center for children under age 3 before first grade?
Did your child attend a pre-primary educational program for children age 3 or older, including kindergarten, before first grade?
Approximately how long was your child in these programs all together (before first grade)?
How old was your child when he/she began the first grade of primary/elementary school?
How far in his/her education do you expect your child to go?
High school diploma (or equivalent) plus technical training or certificate
How often does your child do the following:
Show interest and curiosity in new topics at school?
Work to finish school-related tasks they start?
Care about doing well in school?

ON MOST WEEKDAYS (Monday-Friday), about how much time does your child spend in front of a TV, computer, cellphone, or other device watching programs, playing games, accessing the internet, or using social media? Do not include time spent doing schoolwork.
Does your child have a smartphone?
If yes, at what age did they get it?
Does your child have an account on any social media website such as Instagram, Tiktok, Meta, or others?

We are interested how often, independently, and easily your child shows each behavior when occasions call for it. Children who excel at a specific behavior should receive 4 (the highest rating), whereas most children will be between 3 and 2. Those who are still developing and need a lot of support should receive a rating of 1.

4 = Always on their own (No support needed)
3 = Mostly on their own (Brief reminders or support)
2 = Sometimes on their own (Regular reminders or moderate support needed)
1 = Not able on their own (Requires a lot of support)
<b>Pays attention when an adult is explaining or showing something<b/> (by listening carefully and/or watching with appropriate eye gaze/body orientation).
(4) Always on their own (No support needed)
(3) Mostly on their own (Brief reminders or support)
(2) Sometimes on their own (Regular reminders or moderate support needed)
(1) Not able on their own (Requires a lot of support)
Finishes an assigned task that is repetitive (e.g., picking up toys, putting away laundry).
Stops an undesirable behavior when asked (e.g., stops fighting with sibling when asked by parent, stops misbehaving in the household).
Regains focus/focuses again on an assigned task when interrupted (e.g., family member asks an unrelated question or needs brief assistance).
Waits for their turn to act (e.g., waits patiently in line at a grocery store or when sharing a toy with sibling).
<block><b><font face=Verdana size=4>This section asks about your home environment.
About how many children's books are in your home?
How often does your child eat a meal with family members?
About how many hours is the TV on in your house each day?
Does your family have reliable access to the internet?
How often does your whole family get together with relatives or friends?
Two or three times a month
How often does your child spend time with you or another adult in your home doing outdoor activities?
Once a day or more often
At least four times a week
A few times a year or less
How often do you or another adult in your home get a chance to read stories to your child?
Does your child have access to a tablet or smartphone that they regularly use to watch videos or listen to music?
Does your family have a computer that your child has access to?
How often is your child expected to pick up after him/herself ?
How often does your child read for enjoyment?
Does your family encourage your child to start and keep doing hobbies?
Does your child get special lessons or belong to any organization that encourages activities such as sports, music, art, dance, drama, etc.?
How often is your child expected to help with keeping shared living areas neat and clean?
How often is your child expected to help manage his/her own time (get up on time, be ready for school, etc.)?
How often is your child expected to care for younger family members when you and other adults are not available?
DURING THE PAST MONTH, how often have you felt...
that your child is much harder to care for than most children their age?
that your child does things that really bother you a lot?

These statements describe ways different caregivers act toward their children. Please think about how well each statement describes the way you treat your child. Respond as to whether you do each item "almost never", "once a month", "once a week", "every day".
I see to it that my child knows exactly what (s)he may or may not do.
I always tell my child how (s)he should behave.
I insist that my child do exactly as (s)he is told.
I make my child feel wanted and needed.
I make my child feel what (s)he does is important.
I care about what my child thinks and encourage her/him to talk about it.

All adults use certain ways to teach children the right behavior or to address a behavior problem. Please read the following methods that are used and select whether you or anyone else in your household has used this with your child in the past month.
Took away privileges, forbade something my child liked, or did not allow him/her to leave the house.
Explained why something (the behavior) was wrong.
Do you believe that in order to bring up (raise, educate) your child properly, you need to physically punish him/her?
Sometimes children get so angry at their parents that they say things like I hate you or swear in a temper tantrum. If your child said something like I hate you or swore at you, would you...
Put him/her in a short time out

Thank you for participating in this research project!
The following survey will take approximately 10 minutes to complete and will ask questions about your home and yourself.

We appreciate you answering as completely and accurately as possible with the information you have available to you. If you have questions about this survey, please reach out to the research coordinator.

When you are ready to begin, select the “start survey” button.

Thank you"
Please answer the following questions about the community in which you live.
In your neighborhood, is there a community center or recreation center where families get together?
In your neighborhood, is there a library or other place to get free books?
In your neighborhood, is there litter or garbage on the street or sidewalk?
To what extent do you agree with these statements about your neighborhood or community?
People in your neighborhood help each other out.
People in your neighborhood watch out for each other's children.
Your child is safe in your neighborhood.

These are statements people have made about their food situation. For these statements, please mark whether the statement was often true, sometimes true, or never true for your household in the last 12 months.
The food that we bought just didn't last, and we didn't have enough money to get more.
We couldn't afford to eat balanced meals.
In the last 12 months, did you or other adults in your household ever cut the size of your meals or skip meals because there wasn't enough money for food?
If yes, how often did this happen - almost every month, some months but not every month, or in only 1 or 2 months?
Some months but not every month
Below are statements about the environment in your home. Please indicate how much you agree that the statement is true of your home.
We are usually able to stay on top of things.
You can't hear yourself think in our home.
The atmosphere in our home is calm.
We have a regular morning routine at home.
Over the last two weeks, how often have you been bothered by the following problems?
More than half of the days
Not being able to stop or control worrying
Little interest or pleasure in doing things
We are interested in how you feel about the following statements. Read each statement carefully. Indicate how you feel about each statement.
There is a special person (ex: spouse, partner, etc.) who is around when I am in need.
There is a special person (ex: spouse, partner, etc.) with whom I can share joys and sorrows.
I get the emotional help & support I need from my family.
I have a special person (ex: spouse, partner, etc.) who is a real source of comfort to me.
My friends really try to help me.
I can count on my friends when things go wrong.
I can talk about my problems with my family.
I have friends with whom I can share my joys and sorrows.
My family is willing to help me make decisions.
In your day-to-day life how often have any of the following things happened to you?
You are treated with less courtesy or respect than other people.
People act as if they think you are not smart.
Please select Yes or No to indicate whether each event has happened to you in the past year.
An injury or illness which kept you in bed a week or more, or sent you to the hospital.
Change to a new type of work.
Loss of a job (laid off from work).
Loss of a job (fired from work).
Major change in health or behavior of family member.
Gain of a new family member (birth of a child).
Gain of a new family member (adoption of a child).
Gain of a new family member (a relative moving in with you).
Change to the marital status of your parents (divorce).
Change to the marital status of your parents (remarriage).
Separation from spouse (due to marital problems).
Death of other family member (brother or sister).
Major decision regarding your immediate future.
Major change in finances (investment and/or credit difficulties).
Foreclosure on a mortgage or loan.

Instructions: Think of this line as representing where people stand in the country where you live. To the right are the people who are the best off - those who have the most money, the most education, and the most respected jobs. To the left are the people who are the worst off - those who have the least money, the least education, the least respected jobs, or no job. The further to the right you are on the line, the closer you are to the people at the very top; the further to the left you are, the closer you are to the people at the very bottom.
Where would you place yourself on this line?
Move the slider to where you think you stand at this time in your life relative to other people in your country.

Instructions: Think of this line as representing where people stand in their communities. People define community in different ways; please define it in whatever way is most meaningful to you. To the right are people who have the highest standing in their community. To the left are the people who have the lowest standing in their community.
Where would you place yourself on this line?
Move the slider to where you think you stand at this time in your life relative to other people in your community.

This is Part B of the survey.

In Part B the questions are classroom specific. If you teach more than one class of students included in this project, you will complete Part B separately for each classroom.

If you teach a single subject, please complete the questions that relate to other subjects to the best of your knowledge or consult with a colleague for those details only.

When you are ready to begin, select the “Next” button.

Thank you!
PART A: The following questions ask about your background, the school where you teach, your feelings about your job, and your beliefs about teaching and children.
This set of questions asks about your background.
What is the highest level of education you have completed?
High school diploma or equivalent, plus technical training or certificate
Indicate your undergraduate field of study. Mark ALL that apply.
Other education related major (ex: secondary education, educational psychology, education administration, music education, etc.). Please specify:
Please specify other education related major:
Indicate the major field of study of your highest graduate degree. Mark ALL that apply.
Other education related major (ex: secondary education, educational psychology, education administration, music education, etc.). Please specify:
Please specify other education related major:
Counting this school year, how many years have you been a schoolteacher? (please round to the nearest year)
This set of questions asks about the school where you currently teach.
Counting this school year, how many years have you been teaching at this school? (please round to the nearest year)
Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements about this school.
I feel accepted and respected as a colleague by most staff members.
Parents are supportive of school staff.
Students feel supported and comfortable in this school.
Staff feel supported and comfortable in this school.
Students are safe when here at this school.
Staff are safe when here at this school.
The facilities and property of this school are kept up well.
The facilities and property of this school are clean.
Ongoing collaboration is something adults in this school do well.
Adults working in this school have strong professional relationships.
Adults working in this school are respectful and supportive of one another.
With this set of items, we wish to learn more about teachers' feelings about their job.
Please select one answer that indicates how often you agree with each of the following statements.
A few times a year or less
I feel emotionally drained from my work.
I feel fatigued when I get up in the morning and have to face another day on the job.
I can easily understand how my students feel about things.
I deal very effectively with the problems of my students.
I feel burned out from my work.
I feel I'm positively influencing other people's lives through my work.
I feel frustrated by my job.
I feel I'm working too hard on my job.
I can easily create a relaxed atmosphere with my students.
I feel exhilarated after working closely with my students.
I have accomplished many worthwhile things in this job.
I feel like I'm at the end of my rope.
In my work, I deal with emotional problems very calmly.
This set of questions is designed to help us gain a better understanding of the kinds of things that create difficulties for teachers in their school activities.
on disruptive behavior in the classroom?
to motivate children who show low interest in activities?
to get children to believe they can do well on activities?
to help children in your classroom(s) value learning?
on getting children to follow classroom rules?
on calming a child who is disruptive or noisy?
use a variety of assessment strategies?
provide alternative explanations or examples when children are confused?
assist families in helping their children do well in school?
implement alternative strategies in your classroom(s)?
create good questions for children in your classroom(s)?
With this set of items, we wish to learn more about teachers' ideas and beliefs about children.
Please select one answer that indicates how strongly you agree with each of the following statements.
Since parents lack special training in education, they should not question the teacher's teaching methods.
Children should be treated the same regardless of differences among them.
Children should always obey the teacher.
Preparing for the future is more important for a child than enjoying today.
Children will not do the right thing unless they must.
Children should be allowed to disagree with their parents if they feel their own ideas are better.
Children should be kept busy with work and study at home and at school.
The major goal of education is to put basic information into the minds of the children.
In order to be fair, a teacher must treat all children alike.
The most important thing to teach children is absolute obedience to whoever is in authority.
Children learn best by doing things themselves rather than listening to others.
Children must be carefully trained early in life or their natural impulses will make them unmanageable.
Children have a right to their own point of view and should be allowed to express it.
Children's learning results mainly from being presented basic information again and again.
Children like to teach other children.
The most important thing to teach children is absolute obedience to parents.
